greeting: Morning, Haniel. You had commits landing past 2am — that's the kind of run you finish, not park.
hype: GS has an overdue RBAC call and a full data-foundation queue. Module is one UI away from unlocking Phase 3. Clear both and you end today two moves closer to shipping two different clients.
notice: CT Pros go-live is fully unblocked (company info, QB OAuth, catalog swap, deploy) — all HIGH, no dependencies. Flag it for your next available session.

tasks.title: Apply owner’s 2026-06-24 RBAC decisions
tasks.notes: Due 2026-06-24 (yesterday). Finalizes the permission matrix and unblocks t654/t655/t656.

prep.what: Lock in the owner’s finalized permission decisions from the June 24 review into the codebase, unblocking all RBAC regression tests.
prep.understand: The permission matrix maps roles to permissions across every GS-ERP module. The owner made specific allow/deny calls on 2026-06-24 (captured in t651/t653 context). Until these are applied to the seeder/config, t654 (regression lock), t655 (E2E allow-direction fixtures), and t656 (nav→route consistency) are all blocked.
approach: Find the June 24 RBAC decision log — check t651/t653 task notes, Slack, or any doc from that session
Open the permission seeder or role-permission config in GS-ERP
Apply each decision (role → permission: allow or deny) exactly as recorded
Run the RBAC test suite to confirm no unexpected regressions
resources: Owner’s 2026-06-24 RBAC decision notes (linked in t651/t653)
Permission seeder / role-permission config in GS-ERP repo
Phase 80 task chain: t654, t655, t656
Existing role/permission test suite

tasks.title: Bonus formula definition (production + paint shop)
tasks.notes: Gate before t38 (production bonus system) can be coded. Defines the actual math the system will run.

prep.what: Define and document the bonus formula for production workers and paint shop employees so t38 can implement it in code.
prep.understand: t38 (production bonus system, HIGH, l) is explicitly blocked until this formula exists. You need: what inputs drive the bonus (project revenue? units? hours?), how production and paint shop differ, and any tiers or thresholds. This is a spec/doc task, not a coding task.
approach: Pull any existing informal bonus agreement from GS ownership (email, ClickUp note, verbal spec)
Draft the formula: inputs → calculation → output (dollar amount or % of project value per worker)
Specify production formula and paint shop formula separately if they differ
Write it as a committed spec doc in the repo (e.g., `docs/bonus-formula.md`) so t38 can reference it directly
resources: GS ownership communications about current bonus structure
t38 task notes (production bonus system) for implementation context
Existing labor cost and payroll data in the ERP for formula sanity-checking

tasks.title: Design + installation time benchmarks
tasks.notes: Standard time estimates per job type for design and installation phases — the data layer under capacity planning.

prep.what: Capture how long each job category actually takes (design hours, install hours per unit/sqft) and encode that in the ERP so scheduling is grounded in reality.
prep.understand: Without time benchmarks, the system can’t compute project timelines or flag overload. This is reference data, not a feature build: you’re pulling real-world averages from GS’s history and entering them where the scheduling module reads them.
approach: List the job types in GS-ERP (cabinet, countertop, flooring, etc.) and their phases (design, fab, install)
Pull 3–5 recent completed projects from QB or the CRM to estimate actuals per type
Define benchmark values: design hours per project type, install hours per unit or sqft
Enter them in the settings table or seed file the scheduling module reads
resources: Historical project data in QuickBooks (completed estimates + time records)
GS’s ClickUp history for actual project durations
Scheduling module settings table in GS-ERP codebase
GS production manager’s knowledge of typical job durations

tasks.title: QB integration mapping documentation
tasks.notes: Written field-by-field mapping for how CRM entities map to QuickBooks — the reference for debugging QB sync.

prep.what: Write the field-level mapping between GS-ERP records and QuickBooks entities so sync issues can be diagnosed without diving into source code.
prep.understand: The QB sync is live but undocumented. Every time something breaks, you’re back reading the QBSync classes cold. A mapping doc is a table: CRM entity → QB entity, CRM field → QB field, transformation rules noted.
approach: Open the QBSync service classes in GS-ERP (QBCustomerSync, QBEstimateSync, QBInvoiceSync, QBVendorSync, QBPaymentSyncer, QBExpenseSync)
For each entity: list CRM field → QB field in a markdown table, note any status/type transformations
Add a ‘Known edge cases’ section (e.g., 6240 dup-DisplayName recovery, TxnStatus=Accepted on approval)
Commit to `docs/qb-mapping.md` in the GS-ERP repo
resources: GS-ERP QB sync classes (`app/Services/QuickBooks/`)
QuickBooks API docs (Intuit Developer — entity schemas for Customer, Estimate, Invoice, Vendor)
CT Pros QB sync code as a reference (same architecture, already battle-tested)

tasks.title: CK1/CK2/CK3/CK4 inspection item lists
tasks.notes: Define the checklist items for each quality checkpoint gate (CK1–CK4) in the production process.

prep.what: Create the quality checklist items for each of the four production checkpoints so inspectors have a clear pass/fail list at every stage.
prep.understand: CK1–CK4 are GS’s quality gates through production (likely: CK1=raw material intake, CK2=mid-production, CK3=finishing/QC, CK4=pre-install/delivery). Each needs concrete binary items. This is input data for the inspection module — not a coding task.
approach: Confirm what CK1–CK4 map to with GS (if unclear, check existing ClickUp tasks or ask ownership)
Pull any existing paper checklists or SOPs GS currently uses on the floor
Write each item as a clear pass/fail check (e.g., ‘Wood grain direction matches spec: YES/NO’)
Enter items into the inspection module’s data model (seeder or settings table)
resources: GS’s existing production SOPs or paper checklists
Inspection module in GS-ERP (CK model, admin UI, seeder)
ClickUp historical tasks for what inspectors actually checked

tasks.title: Production capacity/concurrent project limits
tasks.notes: Define and configure the max concurrent projects the production floor can handle — feeds capacity planning and overload warnings.

prep.what: Set the production floor’s concurrent project cap in the ERP so scheduling can warn when GS is overbooked.
prep.understand: This is a configuration task: get the real number from GS (how many projects can run in parallel without quality or deadline risk?), then enter it in the settings key the capacity planning module reads. The feature (t52) already reads this limit — the data just isn’t set yet.
approach: Get the actual concurrent project limit from GS’s production manager or check ClickUp load history
Find the capacity limit setting in GS-ERP (likely a `production_max_concurrent` settings key)
Set the value and confirm the overload warning triggers when it’s exceeded
Document the business logic (e.g., ‘max 4 active cabinet jobs, no cap on small repairs’)
resources: GS production manager’s input or ClickUp project load history
Capacity planning module in GS-ERP (settings key, overload detection logic)
t52 (capacity planning feature) task notes for how the limit is consumed

tasks.title: Owner notification panel for revision_runaway
tasks.notes: Dashboard panel alerting the owner when a project hits revision_runaway — early intervention before scope creep becomes a cost problem.

prep.what: Add an owner-facing panel that surfaces projects in `revision_runaway` state so they can intervene before it becomes a cost problem.
prep.understand: `revision_runaway` is a flag/state on the Project model that triggers when revision cycles exceed a threshold (signals scope creep or communication breakdown). The owner needs this at a glance — not buried in project detail pages.
approach: Find the `revision_runaway` field/event in GS-ERP (Project model, how/when it’s set)
Build a compact panel component: list of projects with `revision_runaway=true` showing project name, client, revision count
Wire it into the owner’s dashboard or notification panel (wherever owner-level alerts live)
Test: set a project to `revision_runaway` and verify it appears in the panel
resources: `revision_runaway` field definition in GS-ERP Project model and related events
Owner dashboard / notification panel code in GS-ERP
Livewire component patterns already used in GS-ERP for similar dashboard widgets

tasks.title: Per-module settings UI (labels, statuses, options)
tasks.notes: Config form per module reading the settings layer — rename labels, define statuses/pipeline stages, edit seedable option lists. Zero-hardcode in practice.

prep.what: Build the per-module settings form inside the Module Manager so super-admins can configure each module’s labels, statuses, and option lists without touching code.
prep.understand: The settings layer (t2) is already live as a keyed store scoped global or per-module. This UI is the front-end: it reads the settings schema for each module and renders an editable form. t8 (module manager grid) is the parent screen to extend. Completing t9 makes t11 (UX-max pass) eligible and closes Phase 2 — which unlocks Phase 3 (Clients module, first real proof of the spine).
approach: Study the settings layer (t2): how settings are keyed per-module, what value types exist (string, list, pipeline-stages)
Add a ‘Settings’ tab or section to the existing module manager card (t8’s Livewire component)
Render grouped form fields: label inputs (text), status pipelines (sortable list), option lists (tag-style CRUD)
Wire save → `SettingsService` with super-admin-only policy gate; show inline success/error feedback
resources: Settings layer (t2): `SettingsService`, `Setting` model, per-module scope API
Module manager UI (t8): existing Livewire component + blade view to extend
Branding settings UI (t10) as a reference implementation for the settings-layer pattern
UX KB: `.claude/docs/ux-kb/` for settings screen conventions
